metadata.title: The Most Dangerous Shape: What a Reallocation Engine Is
metadata.playlist: Build It, Then Doubt It — The Skeptical Reallocation Tool
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Computational Skepticism for AI — Reallocation Engine (100 pts)

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 1 of 9. This series is about one category of AI tool: a reallocation engine. An engine that takes a scarce resource — capital, staff, inventory, access, attention — and moves it from one place to another based on a model's output. What makes that shape uniquely dangerous is not that it crashes. It is that it acts. A plausible-sounding answer to the wrong question does not produce an error message — it moves the budget, the staff, the credit, the aid. That is the assignment's thesis, stated in episode one and held for every episode after. Let's go.

props.command: Choose your domain + write the Frictional Journal prediction before you build.
props.runningText: accuracy is not trust — a plausible answer to the wrong question moves the resource without an error message…

Timestamped prediction saved before any code is written

beats.act: what a reallocation engine is + the tier framework
beats.narration_text: A reallocation engine takes a scarce resource and applies a decision rule to determine where it should go. The resource can be capital — distributing a budget. Compute — assigning processing power. People — staffing a location. Inventory — dispatching supplies. Attention — showing content to users. Access — granting or restricting entry. What all of these share: the engine acts on the world. A classification model tells you something. A reallocation engine changes something. That difference is why the validation task is harder than the build task. The Tier framework: Tier 1 is build work — write the code, wrangle the data, run the model. Tier 4 and 5 are validation work — supervise the tool, formulate the right question, distinguish correlation from causation, catch the output when it is wrong. The build is what you delegate to AI freely. The validation is the assignment. Every episode in this series is a Tier-4 skill: a check the tool cannot run on itself.

props.artifactHeading: It acts on the world — that's why validation is harder than the build
artifactLines: Resource types: capital · compute · people · inventory · attention · access.
Classification model → tells you something. Reallocation engine → changes something.
Tier 1 (build): write code, wrangle data, run model → delegate to AI freely.
Tier 4/5 (validate): supervise the tool, formulate the right question, catch wrong output.
Every episode is a Tier-4 skill — a check the tool cannot run on itself.
props.sparkLine: Accuracy is not trust. A wrong answer to the right question is survivable. A plausible answer to the wrong question moves the resource.

beats.narration_text: Choose your domain before you write a line of code. The domain is the context in which your engine makes decisions: a budget reallocation in a corporate setting, a staff-scheduling system, a logistics engine for humanitarian aid, attention allocation on a platform, access control for a service. Two notes on domain selection. First: if you choose a domain that affects a person's access, attention, or benefits — healthcare, education, financial credit, public assistance — the assignment adds extra weight to the bias audit and requires domain knowledge in the causal analysis, not just code competence. Only choose that domain if you can catch a failure the tools cannot. Second: data ethics. Use a real public dataset or a documented synthetic one. Document its source and its known limitations. The rule is quality over volume: two hundred clean, understood records where you can trace a failure is better than two hundred thousand opaque records where you cannot. The GIGO gate in Episode 3 formalizes this — the data choice is made now.

props.artifactHeading: Domain choice first — before any code
artifactLines: Domain = context: budget · staff · logistics · attention · access. Choose before coding.
High-stakes domains (access/benefits): extra bias weight + domain knowledge required.
Only choose if: you can catch a failure the tools cannot.
Data: real public or documented synthetic. Document source + known limitations.
Quality over volume: 200 clean understood records > 200,000 opaque ones.
props.sparkLine: The domain choice is a commitment: can you catch the failure the model can't?

beats.narration_text: The Frictional Journal. The Frictional Journal is a graded calibration document — ten points off if it is missing or if the reflection does not match an actual pre-build prediction. It has two parts. The prediction comes first and is written before you build. The prediction names the hardest failure you expect: 'I expect the engine will reallocate on a correlated proxy for the true outcome rather than the outcome itself.' Then a confidence estimate — not a hedge, a number. 'My confidence that the tool will produce a causally valid recommendation is forty percent.' The reflection is written after the assignment is complete: what actually happened compared to your prediction, and where you were right or wrong about your confidence. Calibration — the core skill the course is building — is not being right, but knowing how right you are. Write the prediction now, before Episode 2. The timestamp on it is evidence that the calibration is real.

props.artifactHeading: Predict before you build — confidence as a number, not a hedge
artifactLines: Frictional Journal = graded calibration document. −10 if missing or if reflection has no prediction.
Prediction (written BEFORE the build): name the hardest failure you expect.
Confidence estimate: a number. 'My confidence in causal validity is 40%.'
Reflection (written AFTER): what happened vs prediction, where calibration was accurate.
Calibration = not being right — knowing how right you are. Timestamp is evidence.
props.sparkLine: Write the prediction now. The timestamp proves the calibration is real, not retrospective.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for The Premise. A reallocation engine acts on the world — a plausible answer to the wrong question moves the resource. The build is Tier-1 delegation; the validation is Tier-4 supervising, and that is the assignment. Choose your domain — if it affects access, attention, or benefits, you need domain knowledge to catch failures the tools cannot. Data: quality over volume, documented source, known limitations. The Frictional Journal prediction is written before the build — the hardest failure you expect, plus your confidence as a number. The timestamp is evidence. Episode 2 builds the engine.

props.artifactTitle: The Most Dangerous Shape: What a Reallocation Engine Is
artifactLines: Reallocation engine acts — a wrong answer to the wrong question moves the resource.
Tier 1 = build (delegate). Tier 4/5 = validate (supervise). The assignment is Tier 4/5.
Domain: if it affects access/benefits, domain knowledge is required, not optional.
Data: quality over volume. Real public or documented synthetic. Source documented.
Frictional Journal prediction: hardest failure + confidence number. Timestamped. Before E2.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. The domain selection and prediction prompt. Paste this into Claude and fill in the brackets. The prompt reads: I am building a reallocation engine for INFO 7375. My proposed domain is: [describe the resource being reallocated and the context]. My preliminary dataset idea is: [describe the dataset — real public or documented synthetic — and its approximate size]. Audit this domain for me. First: can I catch a failure with domain knowledge that a tool could not catch? If the domain affects access, attention, or benefits, describe what specific domain knowledge I need and what failure mode to expect. Then draft my Frictional Journal prediction in two parts. Part one, the pre-build prediction: describe the hardest failure I should expect from a reallocation engine in this domain, with one sentence on why it is likely. Part two, the confidence estimate: how causally valid is this type of reallocation likely to be before the causal analysis is run — state it as a percentage with one sentence of reasoning. I will timestamp and save this prediction before I write any code. After the domain and prediction are locked, paste the prediction into a document or a code comment with the date and time. Episode 2 builds the engine from here.

props.segment: The Most Dangerous Shape: What a Reallocation Engine Is
props.command: Domain: [resource + context]. Dataset: [describe + size]. Audit: can I catch a failure the tool can't? → Prediction: hardest failure expected + % confidence in causal validity. Timestamp before any code.
props.runningText: the timestamp proves the calibration is real — save the prediction before you open an editor…
